one_line_takeaway: A Capital One position paper arguing (without new experiments) that differential privacy should protect the calibration parameters of stateful agent-based financial simulators rather than static synthetic output tables, so as to build safe 'gyms' for training and auditing autonomous financial agents.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding. The paper's main claim is normative/architectural: that privacy should be enforced at the level of simulation-environment parameters (input to a stateful ABM) rather than at the level of a static released dataset, because this decouples individual identity from the simulation logic even under unlimited adversarial interaction, and because it supports downstream agent-training use cases (fairness calibration, stress testing, adversarial fraud training) that static synthetic tables cannot support.
core_contribution: This is a 4-page position/vision paper (Capital One, accepted at the ICLR 2026 Financial AI workshop) that does NOT introduce a new algorithm or run any experiments. Its actual contribution is conceptual: it (1) proposes and names 'DP-Seeded Agent-Based Modeling (ABM)' as a distinct paradigm from 'Direct Tabular Synthesis' for privacy-preserving financial data generation, (2) gives a comparative taxonomy (Table 1) contrasting the two paradigms across Logic Source, Temporal State, Privacy Locus, and Primary Utility, and (3) argues that DP-Seeded ABM -- injecting differential privacy into the *input calibration parameters* of a stateful simulator rather than into a static output table -- is the necessary infrastructure ('a safe gym') for training autonomous financial agents, enabling fairness auditing via counterfactual re-parameterization, robustness testing against simulated 'black swan' events, and multi-agent adversarial (fraud-vs-defense) training. The paper also names two open technical problems it says future work must address: 'simulation drift' (compounding of DP noise-induced calibration error over long recursive simulation horizons) and the 'sim-to-real gap' (existing validation metrics like SSE on marginals do not capture interaction/causal fidelity).
threat_model: No formal threat model is developed by this paper itself. It gestures at re-identification/de-anonymization risk (citing Narayanan & Shmatikov 2008) and 'linkage attacks' against ad-hoc anonymization as motivating background, and separately mentions agent memorization of transaction-log identities as a risk of naively applying trading-agent frameworks (FinRL, ABIDES) to retail/fraud data -- but these are asserted as motivation, not analyzed with an adversary model, attack surface, or formal privacy game within this paper.
limitations: The paper explicitly identifies its own open gaps rather than claiming to solve them: (1) 'simulation drift' -- small DP-calibration errors can compound over long recursive time horizons and push the synthetic economy into unrealistic macro states (e.g., hyperinflation), and the paper states future work must develop noise-aware calibration to address this; (2) the existing validation metric used by the one real system it draws on (SSE on marginal distributions, from MoMTSimDP) is stated to be insufficient because it does not capture interaction/causal fidelity (e.g., an agent's sensitivity to transaction fees), and new benchmarks are called for. Beyond the paper's own stated gaps, reviewers should note: it is a workshop position paper with zero original experiments, datasets, or baselines; virtually every concrete technical claim traces to a single external system (MoMTSimDP); and no formal privacy accounting, epsilon budget, or attack evaluation is performed anywhere in the paper.

quantitative_results: 720 sequential simulation steps in MoMTSimDP (cited system, not this paper's own experiment) -- Azamuke et al. 2025
SSE (Sum of Squared Errors) on marginal distributions used as MoMTSimDP's utility validation metric (cited system, not this paper's own experiment) -- Azamuke et al. 2025
